EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper wave dropping south across the Great Lakes and it's associated cold front brought widespread accumulating snow to the region New Year's Eve and into the new year across the ridges of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. Enhanced orographic lift resulted in higher totals across the Laurel Highlands and eastern West Virginia ridges, with relatively high snow ratios between 18:1 and 21:1. Widespread Advisory level snow was also observed across the region, ranging from 2.5 inches to 5.5 inches outside of the ridges of Pennsylvania and West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall, generally exceeding advisory criteria but falling short of warning criteria, fell across the county with amounts around 3 inches and the highest total of 4 inches reported by the public in East Liverpool.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix changed over to snow as temperatures plunged well below freezing behind an arctic front. This created an icy flash freeze environment under a half inch to two inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through with a mixture of sleet and rain changing to a period of snow during the afternoon and early evening hours of January 14th. Snow accumulations were generally an inch or two at best. However, the cold air rushing in with the front and snow allowed the wet pavement to freeze during the afternoon and early evening hours. This resulted in numerous reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event produced 6 to 10 inches of fluffy snow accumulation late January 24th into January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, moderate to heavy at times, developed during the evening hours of January 24th and continued through the early evening hours of January 25th. The snow accumulated to between 6 and 10 inches and was fluffy in nature as temperatures were in the single digits and teens. There was a report of 6.8 inches of total snow accumulation 2 miles north-northeast of Glandorf. Roads were snow covered and slick with numerous reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fatality was partially attributed to cold temperatures on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Darke County coroner informed the NWS office that a fatality occurred on this day, partially attributed to hypothermia. The low temperature that morning at the Darke County airport VES was 21 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing through Ontario dragged a strong cold front west to east across the region in the afternoon hours. Boundary orthogonal bands of snow squalls developed ahead of the front with steep low level lapse rates and non-zero surface-based CAPE. The result was a widespread area of snow bands reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less with gusty wind greater than 35 mph and rapid accumulation on roadways resulting in at least one highway accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS K2G2 measured quarter mile visibility with a wind gust to 38 mph in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under strong 1027 mb high pressure, light wind allowed for efficient radiational cooling and minimum wind chill values to dip to -10 to -20F areawide with some spots below -20F, mostly in sheltered areas and higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values generally ranged from -10 to -20 across the county with the exception of a -21 degree reading at a CWOP site 2 miles west of Washingtonville in the early morning hours of the 31st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values generally ranged from -10 to -15 across the county with the exception of a public reading of -20 degrees in the morning hours of the 31st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chills across the county generally ranged from -15 to -20 degrees with the exception of a -21 degree reading at a mesonet site at Atwood Dam Pool.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure maintained dry weather and light wind across the area. Most saw minimum wind chill values into the positive and negative single digits with the exception of a -26F reading at KPHD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values across the county generally ranged in the negative single digits with the exception of a -26 degree reading at the New Philadelphia Airport ASOS in the morning hours on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of strong low pressure tracked up the eastern seaboard downstream of a digging positive to neutrally tilted trough. Deep moisture through the dendritic growth zone and strong forcing resulted in widespread significant snowfall accumulation greater than 6 across the entire county warning area with amounts generally falling between 10-18. Localized amounts under narrow frontogenetically-forced bands reached up to 22 inches. An intruding mid-level warm nose reduced snow amounts slightly in northern West Virginia and resulted in a period of some mixing of snow with freezing rain and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 7 to 10 inches was reported across the county with the highest total of 12.3 inches reported by a CoCoRaHS observer 3 miles southwest of Mount Pleasant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On strong northwest wind, a lake enhanced band of snow fell over an entrenched arctic airmass. This band stretched in a narrow swath from south of Celina to Xenia to south of Chillicothe, with accumulations generally between 1 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from Saint Paris had 2.7 inches of snow, while the cooperative observer 2 miles east-northeast of Saint Paris measured 1.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On strong northwest wind, a lake enhanced band of snow fell over an entrenched arctic airmass. This band stretched in a narrow swath from south of Celina to Xenia to south of Chillicothe, with accumulations generally between 1 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received from South Charleston with 3 inches of snow, and Springfield with 2.3 inches. The CoCoRaHS observer 3 miles east-northeast of Enon measured 1.5 inches, as did the cooperative observer southwest of New Carlisle and a broadcast media report from Enon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm began Saturday night and continued through Sunday as it passed over an entrenched arctic airmass. A strengthening surface low over the Tennessee Valley combined with upper level dynamics to produce snowfall of up to a foot in some areas. The Dayton airport DAY set its highest 24 hour snowfall on record with 12.4 inches on the 25th, breaking the previous record of 12.2 inches set on January 26, 1978.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 5 miles northeast of Seaman measured 6 inches of snow.

Deep moisture through the dendritic growth zone and strong forcing resulted in widespread significant snowfall accumulation greater than 6 across the entire county warning area with amounts generally falling between 10-18. Localized amounts under narrow frontogenetically-forced bands reached up to 22 inches. An intruding mid-level warm nose reduced snow amounts slightly in northern West Virginia and resulted in a period of some mixing of snow with freezing rain and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 6 to 8 inches was reported across the county with the highest total of 8.8 inches reported by the Public 4 miles southeast of Senecaville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm began Saturday night and continued through Sunday as it passed over an entrenched arctic airmass. A strengthening surface low over the Tennessee Valley combined with upper level dynamics to produce snowfall of up to a foot in some areas. The Dayton airport DAY set its highest 24 hour snowfall on record with 12.4 inches on the 25th, breaking the previous record of 12.2 inches set on January 26, 1978.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 3 miles southeast of Reynoldsburg measured 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought a mix of wintry precipitation to the area from the afternoon of January 24th into the 26th. Precipitation initially overspread the area as snow the evening of the 24th, then transitioned to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain overnight into the morning of the 25th. Portions of northeast KY, southwest VA, and central and southern WV also experienced a period of rain as temperatures warmed slightly during the day. Temperatures plunged back below freezing in the wake of a cold front, with precipitation turning back to snow throughout the area that evening. Precipitation lingered overnight then tapered off the morning of the 26th.||In southeast Ohio, the highest snow amounts, which ranged from around five to ten inches, were observed in Perry, Washington, Athens, Vinton, and Jackson Counties. In addition to a few inches of snow, sleet and freezing rain become more dominant further south. Jackson and Lawrence Counties had the most notable sleet amounts, each coming in with around an inch of accumulation. As for freezing rain, a few tenths of an inch of ice were observed in Lawrence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic outbreak settled into southeast Ohio for the second half of January. This was first initiated on January 20th in the wake of a cold front and a departing winter storm, followed by reinforcing shots of cold air through the last week of January. Most nights during this time featured Extreme Cold Warnings and Cold Weather Advisories in response to the dangerously cold wind chills. Subzero wind chills occurred during most nights in the second half of the month.

A passing clipper system helped southeast Ohio ring in the new year in the form of snow squalls. Shortly after the clock struck midnight on January 1st, snow squalls ventured into the Ohio River Valley and dropped around an inch of snow. Local CoCoRaHS volunteers measured close to two inches of snow on the morning of the 1st in the wake of the squall. One snow squall in particular in Athens County resulted in a brief, sharp drop in visibility with heavy snow and strong wind gusts. This event was short-lived, as the clipper system quickly jetted off the east coast by the middle of the day on the first and high pressure built into the Ohio Valley.
